text: Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) provide a promising source for cartilage tissue regeneration strategies. The use of MSCs for such strategies, however, remains challenging due to the low targeting and low chondrogenic differentiation efficiency of these cells to the desired site. In an attempt to overcome such problems, we propose the use of a magnetoresponsive stem cell spheroid (MR-SCS)-based cartilage recovery platform that allows for precise targeting using an electromagnetic actuation (EMA) system to provide magnetic control and low-frequency electromagnetic field (LF-EMF) to allow for biophysical stimulation to promote chondrogenic differentiation. MR-SCSs were fabricated from mouse-derived MSCs that were labeled with magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) using 3D culture methods, and these particles exhibited no cytotoxicity and did not affect chondrogenic differentiation. Locomotion of MR-SCS that was mediated by the EMA system was successfully demonstrated in 3D phantom and ex vivo models. Additionally, LF-EMF stimulation of MR-SCS resulted in increased expression levels of cartilage specific markers, collagen type II, SOX9, and Aggrecan. Finally, histological evaluation revealed an apparent improvement in the regeneration of cartilage tissue in an ex vivo model of the porcine femur in response to LF-EMF stimulation. These results suggest that our experimental platform consisting of MR-SCSs that are subjected to EMA and EMF stimulation may provide a promising therapeutic system for cartilage tissue regeneration.

text: As the world population increases in age and in life expectancy, joint related disorders such as osteoarthritis (OA) are emerging as a major issue in regard to global health. Approximately 10 % of the world population over the age of 60 suffer from OA [1]. OA is usually accompanied by degradation of articular cartilage, inflammation, thickening of the subchondral bone, and formation of osteophytes [2]. For many years, scientists have attempted to regenerate damaged cartilage in patients with OA. Articular cartilage exhibits a low capacity for self-repair, while other tissues such as bone possess the ability to self-regenerate [3]. Given the low capacity for self-repair in cartilage, several treatments have been developed to aid in the healing of injured articular cartilage. Current methods used to treat damaged cartilage include surgical techniques, such as microfracture and osteochondral grafting, and cell-based techniques, such as autologous stem cell or chondrocyte implantations. To facilitate cartilage regeneration, it is necessary to reconstruct the mature, well structured, and biomechanically compliant hyaline cartilage that is primarily composed of collagen type II (COL II) [4]. Surgical methods, however, are limited in their ability to regenerate hyaline cartilage [5]. These methods instead typically result in the regeneration of fibrocartilage that contains a higher percentage of collagen type I compared to that of collagen type II, while implantation of autologous stem cells or chondrocytes results in the generation of more hyaline-like cartilage [6]. Additionally, surgical treatments can be accompanied by many complications such as inflammation, infection, and implantation failure, and they can also result in the need for revision surgery [7, 8]. In contrast, cell-based therapy can be used to facilitate a direct injection of cells into the sites of damage without the need for surgical procedures, and this can reduce operation time and the risk of complications. Due to these advantages, cell-based therapy using mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) or chondrocytes is now the more prominent treatment for injured cartilage regeneration.

text: tissues and organs [9]. A number of studies have investigated the applicability of MSCs in regenerative medicine for the repair of minor and major cartilage defects, and these studies have reported that MSCs can maintain their properties even after several expansion passages [10]. Although MSCs possess excellent therapeutic advantages for cartilage regeneration, low efficiency of cell delivery and maintenance of these cells at the defect area after intra-articular injection continue to pose problems. Loss of cells may result in low therapeutic effects, and accumulation of these cells at inappropriate locations may result in unwanted lesions that can later become cancerous.

text: In an effort to enhance the targeting efficiency of stem cells, several studies have suggested the use of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) loaded into individual stem cells that can be manipulated by external magnetic fields generated from permanent magnets [11]. Through the use of these permanent magnets, individual MSCs loaded with MNPs can be guided and concentrated at sites of damage to promote cartilage regeneration. To precisely deliver the cells into deep cartilage sites such as the meniscus at the tibia or the back side of femoral cartilage, however, magnetic control with a high degree-of-freedom is required. The electromagnetic actuation (EMA) system that is composed of multiple electromagnetic coils provides one promising method to actively control these cells [12, 13]. As the electromagnetic field strength generated by the EMA system, however, is basically lower than the magnetic field from the permanent magnet, a high enough concentration of MNPs should be loaded into the individual stem cells, and these concentrations may cause cytotoxicity.

text: still some unsolved issues associated with long-term safety and efficacy related to these materials. Scaffolds created using synthetic polymers may exhibit degradation problems, and scaffolds made with natural polymers may cause immunological issues or infection [14]. Additionally, the use of porous scaffolds for cell-based therapy also poses some limitations when used as an optimized delivery system. The procedure and the efficacy of micro-scaffolds that possess highly homogenous pores both tend to vary, and cell loading density tends to be inconsistent [15]. Given these limitations, scaffold-free cell spheroids may provide an excellent alternative cell delivery system due to their safety and simple manufacturing process, as these qualities could lower the unknown risks and simplify the procedures. Additionally, spheroids more accurately represent the in vivo environment that promotes intercellular interaction, and this may be beneficial for cartilage regeneration [16].

text: Currently, stem cell-based tissue engineering strategies that incorporate biochemical and biophysical stimulations to induce cell proliferation and differentiation in damaged tissue have attracted a great deal of attention. Biophysical factors, such as stimulation by low-frequency electromagnetic field (LF-EMF), have been reported to promote the release of adequate growth factors and to induce chondrocyte proliferation, differentiation, and proteoglycan production in damaged cartilage [17]. Additionally, LF-EMF stimulation is a non-invasive therapy that is not known to cause any side effects, allowing this technique to be applied during cell culture and under in vivo conditions [18]. Based on the finding of these previous studies, LF-EMF may provide a useful biophysical approach to promote chondrogenic differentiation and the repair of damaged articular cartilage.

text: Mouse mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) were obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, VA, USA) and cultivated in culture medium composed of Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA), 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA), and 1 % (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin (PS; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA) under 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO₂ conditions. The medium was replaced every 2-3 days, and cells were detached from the culture plate when they became 80% confluent using cell dissociation solution to allow for subculture and for further experiments. MSCs passage of 5-6 were used in the experiments and no more than MSCs passage of 7 were used in the experiments.

text: MNPs. The fluidMAG-D is composed of magnetite core and starch surface coating and prepared as a concentration in suspension of 25 mg/mL. The hydrodynamic size of the fluidMAG-D is about 100 nm from the manufacturer, and the size measured from several research groups is 114 nm ± 1 nm [19, 20]. AlamarBlue Cell viability reagent (Invitrogen, OR, USA) and CellTiter-Glo® 3D cell viability assay (Promega, WI, USA) were used to evaluate mouse MSCs viability on MNPs at day 1, 3, and 7. Low-attachment surfaces techniques were used to generate the MR-SCS. MNP labeled mouse MSCs were dissociated, and 10,000 cells/well were distributed into Corning Ultra-Low Attachment Surface 96-well plates (Corning, NY, USA) and then incubated at 37 °C under humidified 5% CO₂ conditions. The size of the MR-SCS was analyzed by microscopy using ImageJ software available at http://rsb.info.nih.gov/ij/. Uptake and contents of MNP in the MR-SCS was confirmed by Prussian blue staining (Polysciences, Inc., Germany) and ICP-OES analysis. Bio-Transmission electron microscopy (BIO-TEM, JEOL Ltd, Japan) was used to confirm the location of MNPs on cells. Additionally, cell tracker (ThermoFisher, MA, USA) was used to observe morphology, and Live/Dead cell viability assay was used to confirm viability of the MR-SCS.

text: The electromagnetic actuation of the MR-SCS was conducted using the EMA system that consists of eight electromagnetic coils and soft magnetic cores (see Supplementary Materials for more details). The design of the proposed EMA system was based on our previous work [12]. The EMA system can create uniform magnetic fields and gradient magnetic fields of 40 mT and 1.8 T/m, and it can also realize five degrees of freedom (DOF) in regard to magnetic objects. Detailed characterization of the EMA system is explained in the Supplementary Information. To observe the 3D locomotion of the MR-SCS, a microscope (f170, Carl Zeiss, Germany) and a DSLR camera (EOS 600D, CANON, Japan) were introduced as bi-plane imaging devices. A controller based on LabVIEW software (National Instruments, Austin, Texas, USA) and eight power supplies (each four sets of MX 15 and 3001 ix, AMETEK, USA) were used to control the input current of each coil.

text: Mouse MSCs and ultra-low attachment surface 96-well plates were used to fabricate the magnetoresponsive stem cell spheroid (MR-SCS). Mouse MSCs labeled with MNPs were agglomerated together and formed into spherical shapes within 3 days. Microscopy and confocal images were used to determine the morphology of the MR-SCS (Fig. 2a, d). The images confirmed that the MR-SCS uniformly formed into spherical shapes regardless of MNP labeling. As the labeling concentrations of MNP increased, the size of the MR-SCS increased. The average size of MNP unlabeled MR-SCS was 182.5 ± 8.6 μm, and the average size of the 0.5 mg/ml MNP labeled MR-SCS was 291.3 ± 16.4 μm.

text: The effect of MNPs on the viability of mouse MSCs in 2D and 3D culture was analyzed using the AlamarBlue assay, and the range of MNP labeling concentrations was 0-0.5 mg/ml on day 1, 3, and 7. The data obtained for this experiment indicated that the viability of mouse MSC labeled with MNPs up to 0.2 mg/ml in both 2D and 3D culture did not decrease to less than 90% compared to the viability of unlabeled mouse MSCs (control) at day 1, 3, and 7 (Fig. 2b, fig. S1). Cytotoxicity was observed when MNPs were used at concentrations of greater than 0.3 mg/ml in both 2D and 3D culture of mouse MSCs. These data indicated that labeling concentration of less than 0.2 mg/ml of MNPs is biocompatible with mouse MSCs. Thus, less than 0.2 mg/ml of MNP was used as the cell labeling concentration for further experiments. The number of cells per 0.2 mg/ml MNP labeled spheroid were confirmed by with Alamar blue cell viability reagent and CellTiter-Glo® 3D cell viability reagent. Initial seeding number of cells per MNP labeled spheroid was about  $ 1.2 \times 10^{4} $.

text: cell/well and after 24 h about  $ 2.4 \times 10^4 $ cells/well formed in single 3D spheroid (fig. S3). The increase of the number of cells is results of proliferation of the cells. Prussian blue staining was used to confirm the uptake of iron oxide nanoparticles in the MR-SCS (Fig. 2c), and according to inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) results, the Fe content of the MR-SCS labeled with 0.2 mg/ml was  $ 0.69 \mu g/MR $-SCS. Furthermore, fluidMAG-D as MNPs for the formation of MR-SCS is known to have Fe and MNPs concentration and the number of MNPs of 37.5 mg/mL, 25 mg/mL, and  $ 1.8 \times 10^{15}/g $, respectively [19, 20]. Therefore, the MNPs concentration and the number of MNPs for single MR-SCS are estimated to be about  $ 0.46 \mu g $ and about  $ 8.28 \times 10^8 $, respectively. To confirm the location of the MNPs on cells, we used Bio-Transmission electron microscope (Bio-TEM). According to the TEM images (Fig. 2c), we confirmed that MNPs were placed in the cytoplasm of the cells (red arrows) and attached to the surface of the cell membrane (green arrows). MNPs attached to the surface of the cell membrane can be explained why the size of the spheroid increased as the labeling concentration of MNPs increased (Fig. 2a). As the labeling concentrations of MNPs increased, the amount of MNPs attached to the surface of the cell membrane increased so the size of the spheroid also increased. These results also confirmed internalized MNPs did not alter the structural properties of the cells.

text: To evaluate the chondrogenic differentiation of the MR-SCS, Alcian blue staining, immunohistochemical staining of collagen type II, and quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) were performed. For qRT-PCR, collagen type II, SOX9, and Aggrecan were used as chondrogenic marker genes. Both MNP-unlabeled and -labeled MR-SCS were stained with Alcian blue to detect proteoglycan synthesis at weeks 1 and 3 (Fig. 2e and fig. S7). Also, immunohistochemical staining confirmed that both MNP unlabeled and labeled MR-SCS expressed collagen type II after chondrogenic differentiation for 3 weeks (Fig. 2f and fig. S8). According to mRNA expression results, the expression of collagen type II and SOX9 was not significantly different between unlabeled and MNP-labeled MR-SCS, and the expression of Aggrecan by MNP-labeled MR-SCS increased significantly (p<0.05). These results indicated that MNPs did not affect the chondrogenic differentiation of the MR-SCS (Fig. 2g).

The proposed EMA system, which consists of eight electromagnetic coils, can steer the MR-SCS to the desired location in 3D space through the use of independent current control of the coils. linearly increased at fixed magnetic field (40 mT) and changing gradient magnetic fields (0.9 - 1.8 T/m, 0.3 steps). Additionally, its velocity along the z-axis was slower than the velocity along the x-axis, as the moving MR-SCS in the working fluid was influenced by gravitational force. Considering variations in the fluid viscosity, the moving speed of the MR-SCS was observed to decrease proportionally as the fluid viscosity decreased. The results of this experiment verified the magnetic propulsion ability of the proposed MR-SCS. Additionally, in the articulated environment, gravitational force and fluid viscosity are important factors that act in addition to the magnetic field generated by the EMA system to affect the movement of the MR-SCS.
To assess the mobility of the MR-SCS using the proposed EMA system, 3D locomotion of a single MR-SCS was conducted in the 70% glycerol solution to mimic the conditions of synovial fluid (Fig. 3c and video S1, Supporting Information). In this test, for stable manipulation of the MR-SCS in 3D space, gravitational force was compensated for through the use of magnetic fields generated by the EMA system. As shown in Fig. 3c, the MR-SCS underwent an upward motion that could overcome gravity (Fig. 3c-(I)), and the levitated MR-SCS could then move along the rectangular path on the xy plane (Fig. 3c-(II-V)). While traveling on this path, the MR-SCS was steered through the teleoperation control at fixed 40 mT of the magnetic field and 1.4 T/m of the gradient magnetic field. The results of this experiment confirmed that the proposed MR-SCS is capable of 3D manipulation by the external magnetic field generated by the EMA system. Incidentally, the magnetic force of the MR-SCS is sufficient to overcome the gravitational force and the viscosity of the joint synovial fluid.
Based on these basic experiments, the locomotion test was also successfully demonstrated ex vivo (Fig. 3d and video S2, Supporting Information). First, a porcine knee joint possessing a defect formed on the distal condyle was filled with 70% glycerol solution and then placed in the EMA system to allow for manipulation of the MR-SCS. Next, nine MR-SCS were injected into locations distal to the medial condyle defect in the prepared ex vivo model. The injected MR-SCSs formed a

text: Although the proposed EMA system demonstrated the precise 3D targeting delivery of a small number of MR-SCSs, cartilage defects with large size require a large number of MR-SCSs to be injected and manipulated into the joint cavity to fill cartilage defects. In addition, the proposed EMA system can generate magnetic fields along the same direction in the workspace. Hence, the MR-SCSs that spread to the synovial fluid can only move in the same direction and thus cannot be manipulated individually. For this reason, unlike manipulating a small number of spheroids, it is difficult to deliver a large number of spheroids injected into synovial fluid to cartilage defects. As an effective method for delivering large numbers of spheroids to cartilage defect with large size, we introduced a direct spheroids delivery method. In this method, the needle is located close to the defect, and the MR-SCSs injected through this needle move directly to the defect along the magnetic field generated by the EMA system. To verify the delivery method of the MR-SCS, the delivery test of the MR-SCSs was performed on a phantom with 2 mm hole (fig. S14 and video S5, Supporting information). Thirty MR-SCSs were injected near the hole through a pipette and guided to the hole using the magnetic field control of the EMA system. The delivery of MR-SCS by magnetic field using the EMA system showed a high efficiency of  $ 94.67 \pm 5\% $, which is superior to manual operation ( $ 18.50 \pm 18.35\% $). Here, the low performance of manual operation is caused by the fluid vortex formed around the defect by injection of the MR-SCS. Through the delivery test of MR-SCS, we confirmed the availability of MR-SCS delivery using the EMA system for knee cartilage repair.

text: To evaluate the effect of LF-EMF stimulation on chondrogenic differentiation of the MR-SCS, immunohistochemical staining of collagen type II and qRT-PCR were performed after LF-EMF stimulation for 30 min each day for 3 weeks. The confocal images of immunohistochemical staining of collagen type II indicated that LF-EMF stimulation did not affect chondrogenic differentiation of MR-SCS. MR-SCS with or without LF-EMF stimulation expressed collagen type II, indicating that chondrogenic differentiation occurs under either condition (Fig. 4b and fig. S10). For mRNA gene expression, expression of all chondrogenic gene markers, including collagen type II, SOX9, and Aggrecan, in MR-SCS after EMF stimulation was higher than levels observed without LF-EMF stimulation. During differentiation, expression of SOX9 significantly increased at weeks 1 and 2, and the expression of collagen type II significantly increased at weeks 2 and 3. These results indicated that LF-EMF stimulation affects and promotes the chondrogenic differentiation of the MR-SCS (Fig. 4c and fig. S11).

text: In this study, we proposed a novel means of cartilage repair that uses MR-SCS combined with EMA and EMS systems. As a non-invasive surgery, intra-articular injection of stem cells to treat injured cartilage is being actively studies by a number of research groups [24, 25]. A large amount (2.5 × 10⁶ cells/500 μL/cm²) [26] of cells, however, is required to obtain cartilage regeneration effects due to the absence of active targeting. Intra-articular injection of these large amounts of cells can cause side effects such as the creation of loose body scar tissue on the surface of articular cartilage. Recently, several research groups have studied the targeting of MNP-labeled stem cells to lesions using magnetic fields as a means of overcoming these problems [27]. Most of these magnetic stem cell targeting studies, however, were evaluated using magnetically labeled individual cells and a bulky superconducting electromagnet [28]. Indeed, MNP concentration within the single magnetic cell is lower than that of the cell spheroid type, and this creates the need for a powerful EMA system. These single bulky magnet systems could not show 3D actuation and precise targeting of cells into defect sites. Given these shortcomings, in this study, we designed stem cell-based 3D spheroids that were composed of millions of MNP-labeled MSCs to improve magnetic propulsion and targeting accuracy without any cytotoxicity. Additionally, a magnetic actuation system that possesses the capability of 3D locomotion of the MR-SCSs to precisely actuate and deliver them into the defect site was proposed. For stem cell implantation, the maintenance of cell stemness and the differentiation potentials is of primary concern. The traditional cell culture technique using a 2D platform is highly artificial, however, and cannot provide dynamic in vivo environments for cells. This, in turn, can cause abnormal cell physiological behaviors that eventually result in loss of stem

text: cell characteristics and functions [29]. In contrast, the 3D culture of MSCs possesses many advantages over the conventional monolayer culture (2D culture), and 3D culture is more similar to in vivo environments that allow for cell to cell and cell to extracellular matrix (ECM) interactions, nutrient and oxygen gradients, matrix deposition, and gene expression profiles [30]. For this reason, we decide to construct MR-SCS with a diameter of approximately 300  $ \mu $m, and to label it with 0.2 mg/ml of MNP to ensure no cytotoxicity and to allow for intra-articular injection to treat injured cartilage for further in vivo experiments. Morphological results indicated that the size of the MR-SCS increased as the labeling concentrations of MNP increased. According to De Simone et al., the volume of D348 cell spheroids labeled with MNPs dose-dependently increased, and this was similar to the observed results in our study. This is likely due to the concentration-dependent accumulation of MNPs around the cell surface during spheroid formation [31]. A previous study confirmed that after loading MNPs into cells and then analyzing them using TEM, the particles were present in both endosome-like structures and at the surface of cells even after several washing steps [32]. The results of these previous studies provide insight into our observation of dose-dependent size increases of MNPs; however, a significant amount of MNPs inside or on the surface of cells can result in cellular toxicity.

The results of these previous studies provide insight into our observation of dose-dependent size increases of MNPs; however, a significant amount of MNPs inside or on the surface of cells can result in cellular toxicity.

text: Many studies have the reported toxicity issues of MNPs on MSCs, and most of them resulted non- or very low cytotoxicity effects on these cells [33]. According to Riegler et al., cell viability depends on particle concentrations and types [32]. Among the different concentrations and types of MNPs, fluidMAG-D, the nanoparticle that we used in our experiments, resulted in greater than 90% cell viability when used at concentrations of up to 0.7 mg particles/ml media on MSCs. fluidMAG-D is coated with starch, which is a biocompatible compound that results in less toxicity than that of non-coated MNPs [32, 34]. The labeling concentration of MNPs on MSCs used to fabricate the MR-SCS was 0.2 mg particles/mL media, and the final iron content of the single MR-SCS was 0.69

text: It is known that MNPs internalize within cells through several endocytosis pathway, such as such as phagocytosis, micropinocytosis, clathrin- or caveolin-mediated endocytosis, then confine them into lysosomes, and degradation occurs [35, 36]. The properties of MNPs, such sizes, coating materials, charges, etc., are factors that determine the uptake pathway and uptake efficiency of the MNPs. The MNPs size smaller than 200 nm and charged MNPs showed higher rate of cellular uptake compared with MNPs size larger than 200nm, uncoated MNPs and neutral MNP [36, 37]. As already mentioned above, FluidMAG-D is coated with starch, size about 100 nm, and negatively charge on surface which meet all three conditions that increase uptake efficiency of the MNPs on cells. After the cellular uptake, MNPs degraded by the decrease in cell magnetization and appear in ferritin form. Internally, cells store the iron in ferritin form which is iron storage protein and release them during the degradation without toxicity [38]. Most well-known mechanism of cytotoxicity induced by MNPs on cells is production of excess reactive oxygen species (ROS) [39]. When excessive ROS produced by internalized MNPs can lead to cell apoptosis and necrosis. Iron ion (II) release from MNPs internalized in cells can cause the ROS production via reaction with hydrogen peroxide. However, if MNPs degraded and appeared in ferritin form, it will inhibit the ROS production and shows less cytotoxicity [38, 40, 41]. Degradation of MNPs depends on the surface coating materials of MNPs. Currently, many research groups have studied about degradation of MNPs in intracellular environments (acidic condition and iron-chelating agent) [42-44]. FluidMAG-D, MNPs we used in this study is composed of magnetite core and coated with dextran surface with size about 100 nm. Levy et al., compared degradation of MNPs with different types of coating materials such as citrate, dextran, and glucose in mimicked lysosomal environment. As a result, degradability of dextran-coated MNPs was higher than other kinds of coated-MNPs. There are several factors that influence the stability of MNPs, such as molecular weight of coating

text: Additionally, controversy remains regarding the possible inhibitory effect of MNP labeling on chondrogenesis in MSCs [46]. Here, the effect of MNPs on the chondrogenic differentiation of the MR-SCS was evaluated by mRNA level analysis and Alcian blue and immunohistochemical staining of collagen type II after 3 weeks of chondrogenic differentiation. The results revealed that similar chondrogenic differentiation capacities were observed in both unlabeled and labeled MR-SCS, confirming that MNPs do not affect chondrogenic differentiation in the MR-SCS. This finding is supported by several previous studies, where Heymer et al. reported that chondrogenic gene expression was not influenced by SPION compared to that of non-labeled cell pallets, and Jing et al. reported no effect of Fe concentration at 50  $ \mu $g/ml on chondrogenic differentiation using in vitro and in vivo experiments [47, 48].

text: MR-SCS appear as continuous tissue in microscopic images obtained after LF-EMF stimulation and in un-stimulated samples. After analysis, however, more histological improvements were observed in the LF-EMF stimulation group compared to those observed in the non-EMF stimulated group after 8 weeks of implantation, and this result supports the findings from our in vitro experiments. According to a review paper detailing the synthetic principle of articular cartilage, MSCs from the spheroids may migrate into the mature tissue of the defect area to induce cartilage remodeling and to allow for integration of the cartilage with the newly produced ECM [58]. Consequently, we found that the MR-SCS under LF-EMF stimulation improved the regeneration of defective cartilage tissue at the osteochondral disc; however, we only performed a short-term ex vivo study to demonstrate the feasibility of LF-EMF stimulation as a potential therapeutic tool for cartilage regeneration. Therefore, long-term experiments and in vivo testing should be performed to confirm the process of cartilage defect repair and to determine if the effects of LF-EMFs on cartilage regeneration may only be a temporary phenomenon. Additionally, a combined electromagnetic coil system that both can actuate and stimulate the MR-SCS will be constructed in the near future to provide a total care system for knee cartilage regeneration.

text: Fig. 2. Characterization and chondrogenic differentiation of MR-SCS. (a) Microscopy images and average size of different concentrations of MNP-labeled (0-0.5 mg/ml) MR-SCS. Scale bar = 200 μm. (b) Viability of the MR-SCS labeled with different concentrations of MNPs (0 - 0.5 mg/ml). (c) Prussian staining of unlabeled (control) and 0.2 mg/ml MNP labeled MR-SCS. Sections stained with Prussian blue were counter stained with nuclear fast red (nuclei). Scale bar = 100 μm. TEM image of unlabeled (control) and 0.2 mg/ml MNP labeled MR-SCS. Arrows indicate MNPs (green arrows-surface, red arrow-cytoplasm). Scale bar = 10 μm. (d) Confocal microscopy images and Live/Dead cell images of unlabeled (control) and 0.2 mg/ml MNP labeled MR-SCS. Scale bar = 100 μm. (e) Alcian blue staining, Scale bar = 100 μm, and (f) COL II immunostaining of cryosections of the MR-SCS unlabeled and MNP labeled cells after 3 weeks of chondrogenic differentiation. Sections stained with COL II were counter stained with DAPI (nuclei). Scale bar = 50 μm. (g) mRNA expression of cartilage-specific genes, COL II, SOX9, and AGG, in the MR-SCSs after 3 weeks of chondrogenic differentiation.

text: Fig. 3. Magnetic actuation characterization of MR-SCS. (a) Experimental setup of the EMA system. (b) Velocity of the MR-SCS along the x- and z-axes in different concentrations of glycerol solutions (n = 5). (c) Screenshots of a video showing 3D locomotion of the MR-SCS. (I) Levitation of the MR-SCS along the z-axis; (II - V) 2D locomotion of the levitated MR-SCS along a rectangular path on the xy plane. Scale bar = 500 μm. (d) Time-lapse images of the MR-SCSs during the 3D ex vivo test. Red dashed arrow indicates the path of the MR-SCSs. Scale bars = 2 mm. (e) Schematic illustration of MR-SCS delivery using X-ray imaging. (f) Magnetic manipulation test of MR-SCS under x-ray fluoroscope. Scale bar = 4 mm. (g) Schematic illustration of MR-SCS delivery using arthroscope imaging. (h) Magnetic delivery test of MR-SCS under arthroscope imaging. Red dashed arrow is the path of the MR-SCSs. Scale bar = 4 mm. The time is indicated on each image in the minutes: seconds format.

text: Fig. 4. Effect of LF-EMF stimulation on MR-SCS. (a) Experimental setup of the LF-EMF stimulation system and schematic illustration of the porcine osteochondral defect model. (b) Collagen type II immunostaining of cryosections and (c) mRNA expression of cartilage-specific genes, COL II, SOX9, and AGG, in the MR-SCSs with or without EMF stimulation for 30 min/every day for 3 weeks. Scale bar = 50 μm. (d) H&E and Alcian blue staining of the ex vivo osteochondral defect model with or without EMF stimulation for 30 min/every day for 4 and 8 weeks. Scale bar = 5,000 μm. (e) Area of regenerated tissue of the ex vivo osteochondral defects model with or without EMF stimulation.
